566	should confirm via \texttt{lean\_local\_search} on \texttt{auslander\_buchsbaum} /
567	\texttt{Module.auslanderBuchsbaum} / similar). If absent, the file will:
568	\begin{enumerate}
569	  \item State Theorem~REF as the formula
570	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}_R(M) = \text{depth}(R)\) under the hypotheses
571	    ``\((R, \mathfrak{m})\) Noetherian local, \(M\) finitely generated, \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\),
572	    \(M \neq 0\)''.
573	  \item Prove it by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\) following the structure of the
574	    proof of Theorem~REF above, importing the supporting
575	    Lemma~REF from Mathlib's depth API or
576	    proving it in tandem.
577	  \item Export Corollary~REF as
578	    \texttt{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of\_regular} (or whatever name Mathlib uses if
579	    already present) as the consumer-facing statement.
580	\end{enumerate}
581	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a homological-algebra construction.
582	The chapter is independent of the rest of Route A's geometric infrastructure (cube of
583	abelian varieties, theorem-of-the-square, Albanese universal property), making it a good
584	parallel-work candidate.
585	
586	\section{Out of scope}
587	\label{sec:ab_out_of_scope}
588	
589	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular
590	\(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay corollary as required by A.4.a. The following downstream
591	constructions are explicitly out of scope here:
592	\begin{itemize}
593	  \item \textbf{The A.4.a codim-1-to-codim-2 extension proof itself} --- consumes
594	    Corollary~REF, but the geometric extension argument
595	    lives in its sibling chapter \texttt{Albanese\_CodimExtension.tex} (planned).
596	  \item \textbf{Local-cohomology vanishing \(H^i_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) for \(i < \text{depth}\)}
597	    --- a downstream consequence of Stacks tag 0AVF; planned for its own chapter
598	    \texttt{Albanese\_LocalCohomology.tex} or as part of A.4.a directly.
599	  \item \textbf{Global Cohen--Macaulay schemes / global depth} --- this chapter only treats
600	    the local-ring case. The global-to-local reduction is standard but not packaged here.
601	  \item \textbf{General homological-dimension theory (global dimension, finitistic
602	    dimension)} --- only the local-ring projective-dimension invariant appears here.
603	  \item \textbf{Cohen--Macaulay characterisations via \(\omega_R\), dualising complex,
604	    Gorenstein refinement} --- the formula and its immediate Cohen--Macaulay corollary
605	    suffice for A.4.a; the duality side is not needed.
606	\end{itemize}

25690	we know that the numerical
25691	polynomial $n \mapsto \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m^n/\mathfrak m^{n + 1}$
25692	has degree $d - 1$. By Lemma \ref{lemma-quotient-smaller-d} we
25693	conclude that the surjection $\kappa[X_1, \ldots, X_d]
25694	\to \bigoplus \mathfrak m^n/\mathfrak m^{n + 1}$ is an isomorphism.
25695	\end{proof}
25696	
25697	\begin{lemma}
25698	\label{lemma-regular-domain}
25699	Any regular local ring is a domain.
25700	\end{lemma}
25701	
25702	\begin{proof}
25703	We will use that $\bigcap \mathfrak m^n = 0$
25704	by Lemma \ref{lemma-intersect-powers-ideal-module-zero}.
25705	Let $f, g \in R$ such that $fg = 0$.
25706	Suppose that $f \in \mathfrak m^a$ and
25707	$g \in \mathfrak m^b$, with $a, b$ maximal.
25708	Since $fg = 0 \in \mathfrak m^{a + b + 1}$
25709	we see from the result of Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-graded}
25710	that either $f \in \mathfrak m^{a + 1}$ or
25711	$g \in \mathfrak m^{b + 1}$. Contradiction.
25712	\end{proof}
25713	
25714	\begin{lemma}
25715	\label{lemma-regular-ring-CM}
25716	Let $R$ be a regular local ring and let
25717	$x_1, \ldots, x_d$ be a minimal set of generators
25718	for the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$. Then
25719	$x_1, \ldots, x_d$ is a regular sequence, and
25720	each $R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)$ is a regular local ring
25721	of dimension $d - c$. In particular $R$ is Cohen-Macaulay.
25722	\end{lemma}
25723	
25724	\begin{proof}
25725	Note that $R/x_1R$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$
25726	by Lemma \ref{lemma-one-equation} with $x_2, \ldots, x_d$
25727	generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
25728	Since $R$ is a domain by Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-domain}
25729	$x_1$ is a nonzerodivisor.
25730	\end{proof}
25731	
25732	\begin{lemma}
25733	\label{lemma-regular-quotient-regular}
25734	Let $R$ be a regular local ring. Let $I \subset R$ be an ideal
25735	such that $R/I$ is a regular local ring as well. Then
25736	there exists a minimal set of generators $x_1, \ldots, x_d$
25737	for the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$ of $R$ such that
25738	$I = (x_1, \ldots, x_c)$ for some $0 \leq c \leq d$.
25739	\end{lemma}
25740	
25741	\begin{proof}
25742	Say $\dim(R) = d$ and $\dim(R/I) = d - c$.
25743	Denote $\overline{\mathfrak m} = \mathfrak m/I$ the
25744	maximal ideal of $R/I$. Let $\kappa = R/\mathfrak m$. We have
25745	$$
25746	\dim_\kappa((I + \mathfrak m^2)/\mathfrak m^2) =
25747	\dim_\kappa(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)
25748	- \dim(\overline{\mathfrak m}/\overline{\mathfrak m}^2) = d - (d - c) = c
25749	$$
25750	by the definition of a regular local ring. Hence we can choose
25751	$x_1, \ldots, x_c \in I$ whose images in $\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2$
25752	are linearly independent and supplement with
25753	$x_{c + 1}, \ldots, x_d$ to get a minimal system of generators of
25754	$\mathfrak m$. The induced map $R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c) \to R/I$ is a
25755	surjection between regular local rings of the same dimension
25756	(Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-ring-CM}). It follows that
25757	the kernel is zero, i.e., $I = (x_1, \ldots, x_c)$. Namely, if not
25758	then we would have $\dim(R/I) < \dim(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c))$ by
25759	Lemmas \ref{lemma-regular-domain} and \ref{lemma-one-equation}.
25760	\end{proof}
25761	
25762	\begin{lemma}
25763	\label{lemma-free-mod-x}
25764	Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring.
25765	Let $x \in \mathfrak m$.
25766	Let $M$ be a finite $R$-module such that
25767	$x$ is a nonzerodivisor on $M$ and
25768	$M/xM$ is free over $R/xR$.
25769	Then $M$ is free over $R$.
25770	\end{lemma}
25771	
25772	\begin{proof}
25773	Let $m_1, \ldots, m_r$ be elements of $M$ which map to
25774	a $R/xR$-basis of $M/xM$. By Nakayama's Lemma \ref{lemma-NAK}
25775	$m_1, \ldots, m_r$ generate $M$. If $\sum a_i m_i = 0$
25776	is a relation, then $a_i \in xR$ for all $i$. Hence
25777	$a_i = b_i x$ for some $b_i \in R$. Hence
25778	the kernel $K$ of $R^r \to M$ satisfies $xK = K$
25779	and hence is zero by Nakayama's lemma.

{"symbol":"open CategoryTheory","info":"Makes names from other namespaces visible without writing the namespace prefix.

Names that are made available with `open` are visible within the current `section` or `namespace`
block. This makes referring to (type) definitions and theorems easier, but note that it can also
make [scoped instances], notations, and attributes from a different namespace available.

The `open` command can be used in a few different ways:

* `open Some.Namespace.Path1 Some.Namespace.Path2` makes all non-protected names in
  `Some.Namespace.Path1` and `Some.Namespace.Path2` available without the prefix, so that
  `Some.Namespace.Path1.x` and `Some.Namespace.Path2.y` can be referred to by writing only `x` and
  `y`.

* `open Some.Namespace.Path hiding def1 def2` opens all non-protected names in `Some.Namespace.Path`
  except `def1` and `def2`.

* `open Some.Namespace.Path (def1 def2)` only makes `Some.Namespace.Path.def1` and
  `Some.Namespace.Path.def2` available without the full prefix, so `Some.Namespace.Path.def3` would
  be unaffected.

  This works even if `def1` and `def2` are `protected`.

* `open Some.Namespace.Path renaming def1 → def1', def2 → def2'` same as `open Some.Namespace.Path
  (def1 def2)` but `def1`/`def2`'s names are changed to `def1'`/`def2'`.

  This works even if `def1` and `def2` are `protected`.

* `open scoped Some.Namespace.Path1 Some.Namespace.Path2` **only** opens [scoped instances],
  notations, and attributes from `Namespace1` and `Namespace2`; it does **not** make any other name
  available.

* `open <any of the open shapes above> in` makes the names `open`-ed visible only in the next
  command or expression.

[scoped instance]: https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/type_classes.html#scoped-instances
(Scoped instances in Theorem Proving in Lean)


## Examples

/-- SKI combinators https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SKI_combinator_calculus -/
namespace Combinator.Calculus
  def I (a : α) : α := a
  def K (a : α) : β → α := fun _ => a
  def S (x : α → β → γ) (y : α → β) (z : α) : γ := x z (y z)
end Combinator.Calculus

section
  -- open everything under `Combinator.Calculus`, *i.e.* `I`, `K` and `S`,
  -- until the section ends
  open Combinator.Calculus

  theorem SKx_eq_K : S K x = I := rfl
end

-- open everything under `Combinator.Calculus` only for the next command (the next `theorem`, here)
open Combinator.Calculus in
theorem SKx_eq_K' : S K x = I := rfl

section
  -- open only `S` and `K` under `Combinator.Calculus`
  open Combinator.Calculus (S K)

  theorem SKxy_eq_y : S K x y = y := rfl

  -- `I` is not in scope, we have to use its full path
  theorem SKxy_eq_Iy : S K x y = Combinator.Calculus.I y := rfl
end

section
  open Combinator.Calculus
    renaming
      I → identity,
      K → konstant

  #check identity
  #check konstant
end

section
  open Combinator.Calculus
    hiding S

  #check I
  #check K
end

section
  namespace Demo
    inductive MyType
    | val

    namespace N1
      scoped infix:68 " ≋ " => BEq.beq

      scoped instance : BEq MyType where
        beq _ _ := true

      def Alias := MyType
    end N1
  end Demo

  -- bring `≋` and the instance in scope, but not `Alias`
  open scoped Demo.N1

  #check Demo.MyType.val == Demo.MyType.val
  #check Demo.MyType.val ≋ Demo.MyType.val
  -- #check Alias -- unknown identifier 'Alias'
end","diagnostics":[]}

  ext r
  simp only [Submodule.mem_bot, Module.mem_annihilator]
  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, fun h s => ?_⟩
  · have := h 1
    simpa using this
  · simp [h]

example {R : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]

  have : Module.annihilator R R = ⊥ := by

    simp only [Submodule.mem_bot, Module.mem_annihilator]
    refine ⟨fun h => ?_, fun h s => ?_⟩

  have hp' : p ∈ (Module.annihilator R R).minimalPrimes := by

import Mathlib
#check @IsAssociatedPrime
example {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (I : Ideal R) (h : IsAssociatedPrime I R) : True := by
  unfold IsAssociatedPrime at h
  -- h : I.IsPrime ∧ ∃ x, I = (Submodule.span R {x}).annihilator
  obtain ⟨hp, x, hx⟩ := h
  trivial

import Mathlib
-- Goal: from (Ideal.span {x}) ∈ minimalPrimes R, extract y ≠ 0 with x · y = 0.
-- Try: minimal prime is associated, so ∃ y : R, (Ideal.span {x}) = Ann(span R {y}),
-- meaning x · y = 0 and Ann(span R y) = (x), but in particular y need not be 0.
-- Wait: if I = Ann(span R {y}) is a prime, then it's proper, so 1 ∉ I.
-- That means 1 ∉ Ann(y), i.e., 1 · y ≠ 0, i.e., y ≠ 0.
example {R : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R] {x : R}
    (hp : Ideal.span {x} ∈ minimalPrimes R) :
    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ x * y = 0 := by
  have hMA : Module.annihilator R R = ⊥ := by
    ext r
    simp only [Submodule.mem_bot, Module.mem_annihilator]
    refine ⟨fun h => ?_, fun h s => ?_⟩
    · have := h 1; simpa using this
    · simp [h]
  have hp' : (Ideal.span {x}) ∈ (Module.annihilator R R).minimalPrimes := by
    rw [hMA]; exact hp
  have hass :=
    Module.associatedPrimes.minimalPrimes_annihilator_subset_associatedPrimes R R hp'
  -- IsAssociatedPrime (span {x}) R: ∃ y, span {x} = Ann(span {y}).
  obtain ⟨hP, y, hy⟩ := hass
  refine ⟨y, ?_, ?_⟩
  · intro hy0
    subst hy0
    -- Now span {x} = Ann(span {0}) = Ann(⊥) = ⊤
    rw [Submodule.span_zero_singleton, Submodule.annihilator_bot] at hy
    exact hP.ne_top hy
  · -- x ∈ span {x} = Ann(span {y}), so x · y = 0.
    have hxIn : x ∈ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R) := Ideal.subset_span (Set.mem_singleton _)
    rw [hy] at hxIn
    rw [Submodule.mem_annihilator] at hxIn
    exact hxIn y (Submodule.subset_span (Set.mem_singleton _))

input.code:
import Mathlib

example {R : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R] {x : R}
    (hp : Ideal.span {x} ∈ minimalPrimes R) :
    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ Ideal.span {x} = (⊥ : Submodule R R).colon {y} := by
  have hMA : Module.annihilator R R = ⊥ := by
    ext r
    simp only [Submodule.mem_bot, Module.mem_annihilator]
    refine ⟨fun h => ?_, fun h s => ?_⟩
    · have := h 1; simpa using this
    · simp [h]
  have hp' : (Ideal.span {x}) ∈ (Module.annihilator R R).minimalPrimes := by
    rw [hMA]; exact hp
  have hass :=
    Module.associatedPrimes.minimalPrimes_annihilator_subset_associatedPrimes R R hp'
  rw [AssociatedPrimes.mem_iff, isAssociatedPrime_iff] at hass
  obtain ⟨hP, y, hy⟩ := hass
  refine ⟨y, ?_, hy⟩
  intro hy0
  subst hy0
  -- now I = (⊥ : Submodule R R).colon {0} = ⊤
  apply hP.ne_top
  rw [hy]
  ext r
  simp [Submodule.mem_colon, Submodule.mem_bot]

private lemma exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrime
    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] {𝔭 : Ideal R} (h𝔭 : 𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R)
    {x : R} (hx : x ∈ 𝔭) :
    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ x * y = 0 := by
  have hdisj : Disjoint (𝔭 : Set R) (nonZeroDivisors R : Set R) :=
    Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes h𝔭
  have hxNot : x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R := fun hxNZD =>
    (Set.disjoint_left.mp hdisj) hx hxNZD
  rw [mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff_exists] at hxNot
  push_neg at hxNot
  -- result: ∃ y ≠ 0, x * y = 0
  -- the original form might be `∀ y, x * y = 0 → y = 0`; with push_neg: `∃ y, x * y = 0 ∧ y ≠ 0`.

  mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff

import Mathlib

example {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (x : R) :
    x ∈ nonZeroDivisors R ↔ ∀ z, x * z = 0 → z = 0 := by
  refine ⟨fun h z hz => ?_, fun h => ?_⟩
  · exact (mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff.mp h).1 z hz
  · rw [mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff]
    refine ⟨h, ?_⟩
    intro z hz
    apply h z
    rw [mul_comm]; exact hz

private lemma exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrime
    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] {𝔭 : Ideal R} (h𝔭 : 𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R)
    {x : R} (hx : x ∈ 𝔭) :
    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ x * y = 0 := by
  have hdisj : Disjoint (𝔭 : Set R) (nonZeroDivisors R : Set R) :=
    Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes h𝔭
  have hxNot : x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R := fun hxNZD =>
    (Set.disjoint_left.mp hdisj) hx hxNZD
  -- ¬ (x ∈ nonZeroDivisors R) ↔ ¬ ∀ z, x*z = 0 → z = 0 ↔ ∃ z, x*z = 0 ∧ z ≠ 0.
  have hExistsZD : ¬ ∀ z, x * z = 0 → z = 0 := fun h => hxNot <| by
    rw [mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff]
    refine ⟨h, fun z hz => ?_⟩
    apply h z; rw [mul_comm]; exact hz
  push_neg at hExistsZD
  obtain ⟨y, hxy, hy⟩ := hExistsZD
  exact ⟨y, hy, hxy⟩

/-! ### Helper iter-191 Lane G (axiom-clean): zero-divisor witness from a minimal prime

For a commutative ring `R` and a minimal prime `𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R`, every
element of `𝔭` is a zero-divisor in `R`. Concretely: for any `x ∈ 𝔭`, there
exists `y ∈ R, y ≠ 0` with `x * y = 0`.

Proof: minimal primes are disjoint from non-zero-divisors via
`Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes`, so `x ∈ 𝔭` ⟹
`x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R` ⟹ `∃ y ≠ 0, x * y = 0`.

This packages the Stacks-00NQ-relevant first step for the `x ∈ 𝔭` case of
`isDomain_of_regularLocal`: when `(x)` is a minimal prime, `x` is a zero-divisor,
so the obstruction reduces to derivation of a contradiction from the
zero-divisor witness (using the regular-local structure of `R` and `R/(x)`). -/
private lemma exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes
    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] {𝔭 : Ideal R} (h𝔭 : 𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R)
    {x : R} (hx : x ∈ 𝔭) :
    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ x * y = 0 := by
  have hdisj : Disjoint (𝔭 : Set R) (nonZeroDivisors R : Set R) :=
    Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes h𝔭
  have hxNot : x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R := fun hxNZD =>
    (Set.disjoint_left.mp hdisj) hx hxNZD
  have hExistsZD : ¬ ∀ z, x * z = 0 → z = 0 := fun h => hxNot <| by
    rw [mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff]
    refine ⟨h, fun z hz => h z (by rw [mul_comm]; exact hz)⟩
  push Not at hExistsZD
  obtain ⟨y, hxy, hy⟩ := hExistsZD
  exact ⟨y, hy, hxy⟩

/-! ### Helper iter-191 Lane G (substantive typed sorry): `(x)` is not a minimal prime
    in the regular-local inductive step

For a regular local Noetherian ring `R` of `spanFinrank 𝔪 = k + 1 ≥ 1` and
`x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, the ideal `Ideal.span {x}` is *not* a minimal prime of `R`.
This is the substantive remaining content of Stacks 00NQ after the iter-190
case-split scaffold isolated the obstruction to this single case.

**Statement framing.** The lemma takes the strong-induction hypothesis
`hIH` as an explicit argument (universally quantified over the ring `R'` at
dimension `k`), so it can be invoked inside `isDomain_of_regularLocal`'s
`succ` arm without requiring `IsDomain R` (which is the goal we are
proving). Concretely `hIH` provides `IsDomain (R / (x))` since the quotient
is regular local of `spanFinrank = k` (via
`regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq`).

**Proof routes (iter-192+ targets).** Three project-side routes have been
considered, all blocked on substrate work beyond Mathlib `b80f227`:
* (i) **Graded-ring approach** (the Stacks 00NQ proof). Build the associated
  graded ring `gr_𝔪 R := ⨁_n 𝔪^n / 𝔪^{n+1}`; prove the surjection
  `κ[X_1,…,X_{k+1}] ↠ gr_𝔪 R` (from a regular system of parameters) is an
  isomorphism (via Hilbert-Samuel function or cotangent count); deduce
  `gr_𝔪 R` is a domain; conclude `R` is a domain via leading-term
  multiplicativity + Krull intersection. ~500–800 LOC.
* (ii) **Cohen-completion bridge.** Pass to `R̂` at `𝔪`, which is regular
  local of same dimension. By the Cohen structure theorem,
  `R̂ ≅ κ̂[[T_1,…,T_{k+1}]]` is a power-series ring over a complete field,
  hence a domain. By flatness of `R → R̂`, the injection `R ↪ R̂` shows `R`
  is a domain. ~400–600 LOC (Cohen structure thm is the big substrate
  cost).
* (iii) **Direct prime-avoidance + Krull-intersection contradiction.** From
  the zero-divisor witness `y ≠ 0, x * y = 0` (via
  `exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes`) and Krull intersection
  `⋂_n (x)^n = ⊥`, write `y = x^m * z` with `z ∉ (x)` and `m < ∞`. Then
  `x^{m+1} * z = 0` and, since `R/(x)` is a domain by IH, `z` represents a
  non-zero-divisor in `R/(x)`. The remaining substantive step is a
  "pull-back" / lifting argument showing `x` must be regular on `R`,
  contradicting the zero-divisor witness. Estimated ~200–300 LOC modulo
  Mathlib's regular-sequence-via-localization-and-completion infrastructure.

iter-191 Lane G lands the *structural extraction* of this lemma: the typed
sorry is now narrow and well-typed, with all dependent instances in scope,
ready for a future iter's substrate work. The HARD BAR iter-191 outcome —
`exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes` axiom-clean — is the
*first* helper unlocking route (iii). -/
private lemma notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ
    (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
    [IsRegularLocalRing R] {k : ℕ}
    (hdim : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = k + 1)
    (x : R) (hxMem : x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
    (hxNotSq : x ∉ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ^ 2)
    (hIH : ∀ (R' : Type u) [CommRing R'] [IsLocalRing R'] [IsNoetherianRing R']
            [IsRegularLocalRing R'],
            (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R').spanFinrank = k → IsDomain R') :
    Ideal.span ({x} : Set R) ∉ minimalPrimes R := by
  intro hmin
  -- Step 1: x is a zero-divisor in R via the axiom-clean Helper A.
  have hxIn : x ∈ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R) :=
    Ideal.subset_span (Set.mem_singleton x)
  obtain ⟨y, hy_ne, hxy⟩ :=
    exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes hmin hxIn
  -- Step 2: bring `R/(x)` into scope as a regular local ring of `spanFinrank = k`,
  -- and apply IH to obtain `IsDomain (R/(x))`. This packages the substrate that
  -- routes (i)/(ii)/(iii) all need to close the residual.
  obtain ⟨hNT, hLR, hRLR, hdim_quot⟩ :=
    regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq hdim x hxMem hxNotSq
  haveI : Nontrivial (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) := hNT
  haveI : IsLocalRing (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) := hLR
  haveI : IsRegularLocalRing (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) := hRLR
  haveI hDomain_quot : IsDomain (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) :=
    hIH (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) hdim_quot
  -- Step 3: derive False from the zero-divisor witness `(y, hxy)` and
  -- `IsDomain (R/(x))`. This is the substantive Stacks 00NQ remnant —
  -- routes (i)/(ii)/(iii) detailed in the docstring above.
  -- iter-191 Lane G leaves this step as a focused, well-typed sorry.
  exact absurd hxy (by
    -- The residual content: `x * y = 0 ∧ y ≠ 0` is impossible in our setting.
    -- For now, defer to substantive substrate work.
    intro _
    sorry)

/-- **Stacks 00NQ — regular local Noetherian ring is a domain.** Every regular
local Noetherian ring is an integral domain.

This is the consumer-facing implication needed to close
`exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (and through it `CohenMacaulay.of_regular`).

**iter-190 Lane G project-side build.** Per progress-critic CONVERGING-with-
escalation advisory, we commit to Option (a) project-side proof. The body
is structured as a strong induction on `spanFinrank 𝔪 R` with two named
axiom-clean helpers:

* Base case `n = 0` → `isDomain_of_isLocalRing_of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_zero`
  (𝔪 collapses to `⊥`, R is a field, hence a domain).
* Inductive prep `n = k + 1` → `regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq`
  (picks `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` via `exists_notMemSq_of_spanFinrank_pos`, then R/(x)
  is regular local of `spanFinrank = k`, axiom-clean via cotangent dim-drop
  + Krull-height bound).

**iter-191 Lane G:** The inductive step is now fully structural:
* `x ∉ 𝔭` branch closes axiom-clean via `𝔭 ⊆ x·𝔭` + Nakayama.
* `x ∈ 𝔭` branch reduces to `Ideal.span {x} ∈ minimalPrimes R` being
  impossible, packaged in the typed-sorry helper
  `notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ` (the structurally-narrow
  Stacks 00NQ remnant; iter-192+ scaffolds via route (i)/(ii)/(iii)).

Once `notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ` is closed, this entire
`isDomain_of_regularLocal` body is axiom-clean. -/

# AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean

## Lane G iter-191: Stacks 00NQ `x ∈ 𝔭` case — structural refactor + 1 axiom-clean helper

### Summary

- **HARD BAR met**: 1 axiom-clean helper landed
  (`exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes`), 1 typed-sorry helper
  isolating the residual Stacks 00NQ content
  (`notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ`).
- **Sorry count unchanged** at 2 (was 2 entering iter-191; remains 2
  post-iter). But sorry placement shifted: the `sorry` in
  `isDomain_of_regularLocal` itself is gone; the residual is now structurally
  isolated to the new helper.
- **Axiom budget**: kernel-only axioms (propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound)
  for the axiom-clean helper.
- **Build status**: `lake build AlgebraicJacobian` GREEN (8360/8360 jobs).

### isDomain_of_regularLocal `x ∈ 𝔭` case (was line 1537)

#### Attempt 1 (iter-191 Lane G)

- **Approach**: Structural refactor. Extract two new helpers immediately
  before `isDomain_of_regularLocal`:

  1. **`exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes`** (axiom-clean,
     ~25 LOC): For any commutative ring `R` and minimal prime
     `𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R`, every `x ∈ 𝔭` admits a zero-divisor witness
     `y ≠ 0` with `x * y = 0`. Proof: minimal primes are disjoint from
     non-zero-divisors via `Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes`,
     then `mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff` + `push Not` extracts the witness.

  2. **`notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ`** (typed sorry, narrowly
     scoped): For regular local Noetherian `R` of `spanFinrank 𝔪 = k+1 ≥ 1`,
     `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, and strong-induction hypothesis `hIH` at dimension `k`,
     `Ideal.span {x} ∉ minimalPrimes R`. The body assembles the substrate
     (zero-divisor witness from Helper 1; `R/(x)` instances and
     `IsDomain (R/(x))` from `regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq`
     + `hIH`) and leaves a focused, well-typed sorry for the residual
     contradiction step.

- **Use site update**: `isDomain_of_regularLocal` `x ∈ 𝔭` case now reads:

  ```lean
  have hmin : Ideal.span ({x} : Set R) ∈ minimalPrimes R := h𝔭_eq ▸ h𝔭_min
  exact absurd hmin
    (notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ R hdim x hxMem hxNotSq
      (fun R' _ _ _ _ h => ih R' h))
  ```

  Lemma body is now structurally clean: `isDomain_of_regularLocal` itself
  has no inline `sorry`.

- **Result**: PARTIAL (substrate narrowed; HARD BAR met).
- **Axiom check**: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_verify` confirms
  `exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes` uses only
  `propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound`.

### Next steps (iter-192+ target)

The residual sorry in `notMem_minimalPrimes_of_regularLocal_succ` requires
substantive Stacks 00NQ content. Three project-side routes documented in the
helper's docstring:

- **(i) Graded-ring approach** (Stacks `lemma-regular-graded`): build
  `gr_𝔪 R := ⨁_n 𝔪^n / 𝔪^{n+1}`; prove `κ[X_1,…,X_{k+1}] ↠ gr_𝔪 R` is an
  isomorphism via Hilbert-Samuel / cotangent dim count; deduce `gr_𝔪 R` is
  a domain; conclude via leading-term multiplicativity + Krull intersection.
  ~500–800 LOC.
- **(ii) Cohen-completion bridge**: `R̂` is regular local of same dim, and by
  Cohen structure theorem `R̂ ≅ κ̂[[T_1,…,T_{k+1}]]` is a power-series ring,
  hence a domain; `R ↪ R̂` by faithful flatness. ~400–600 LOC.
- **(iii) Direct prime-avoidance + Krull-intersection**: with the
  zero-divisor witness `y ≠ 0, x * y = 0` already in scope from Helper 1,
  decompose `y = x^m · z` (Krull intersection on `(x) ⊆ 𝔪`) with `z ∉ (x)`.
  Then `x^{m+1} · z = 0` in `R`, `z̄` is a non-zero-divisor in `R/(x)`
  (a domain by IH); the remaining substantive step is a lifting/pull-back
  showing `x` must be regular on `R`. ~200–300 LOC.

iter-191 Lane G unlocks **route (iii)** by landing
`exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes` axiom-clean. iter-192+
target: close the residual via route (iii)'s lifting argument, which only
requires Mathlib's existing Krull-intersection infrastructure
(`Ideal.iInf_pow_eq_bot_of_isLocalRing`) plus localization / completion
substrate.

### Lemmas discovered

- `Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes` (Mathlib): minimal
  primes are disjoint from non-zero-divisors. **Key input** for Helper 1.
- `mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff` (Mathlib): both-sided characterization of
  non-zero-divisors.
- `Ideal.iInf_pow_eq_bot_of_isLocalRing` (Mathlib): Krull intersection
  theorem for local rings. **Substrate** for iter-192+ route (iii).
- `Module.associatedPrimes.minimalPrimes_annihilator_subset_associatedPrimes`
  (Mathlib): alternative path to extract zero-divisor witness (not used in
  final proof since `Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes` is
  more direct).
- `Ideal.height_le_card_of_mem_minimalPrimes_span_finset`,
  `height_le_ringKrullDim_quotient_add_spanFinrank` (Mathlib): Krull-height
  bounds. Investigated for catenary-style argument; doesn't give
  contradiction directly without catenary axiom on `R`.

### Blueprint marker recommendation

No new declarations exposed to the blueprint. The new helpers are `private`
and serve internal proof structure. Existing pins
(`auslander_buchsbaum_formula`, `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`, etc.) remain
typed-sorry-bearing per the `\leanok` policy (sync_leanok will manage).

### File state (line counts approximate)

- Total sorries in file: **2** (was 2 entering iter-191).
- Lines added: ~110 (docstrings + 2 new helpers).
- Build status: GREEN (`lake build AlgebraicJacobian` exit 0).
- Axiom budget: 0 new project axioms; kernel-only on the axiom-clean
  helper.

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

10	/-!
11	# Codimension-1 indeterminacy extension (A.4.a)
12	
13	This file is the **A.4.a** sub-build chapter for the positive-genus arm of the
14	project's `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the two extension inputs to
15	Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §I.3 Theorem 3.2, plus the Weil-divisor
16	reformulation of the codim-`1` obstruction:
17	
18	* **Milne Theorem 3.1** (`extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth`): a rational map
19	  from a nonsingular variety to a complete variety has indeterminacy locus
20	  of codimension `≥ 2`; if additionally the map is already
21	  codim-`1`-indeterminacy-free, then it extends to a regular morphism.
22	* **Milne Lemma 3.3** (`indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`): for a
23	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety, the
24	  indeterminacy locus is either empty or of pure codimension `1`.
25	* **Weil-divisor obstruction** (`mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point`):
26	  `f` is defined at the generic point of a prime divisor `W` iff some
27	  `PartialMap` representative of `f` is regular at `W.point`. This is a thin
28	  re-shuffle of Mathlib's `RationalMap.mem_domain`; the substantive
29	  Hartshorne-II.6 "regular ⇔ order ≥ 0 at every DVR pullback" reformulation
30	  (the original Weil-divisor obstruction) is deferred to a future lemma
31	  that builds genuine pullback machinery (`Scheme.RationalMap.order`-along-`f`),
32	  outside this iter's scope.
33	
34	These outputs feed Milne Theorem 3.2 in the sibling
35	`Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean` (A.4.c) and consume
36	`cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` from `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (A.4.b)
37	at Step 2 of `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth`.
38	
39	## Status (iter-177 Lane 6 file-skeleton)
40	
41	Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations carries the *intended*
42	substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
43	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_CodimOneExtension.tex`) with a `sorry` body.
44	Bodies are iter-178+ work, gated on the local-cohomology / depth-≥2
45	extension lemma (Step 2 of `thm:codim_one_extension`) being supplied either
46	from `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (the `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay`
47	input) or from a Mathlib upstream once the local-cohomology vanishing
48	`H¹_{x}(V, 𝒪_X) = 0` at a depth-≥2 point lands.
49	
50	The 6 pinned declarations are:
51	
52	1. `Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus` (def, ~3 LOC) — the closed
53	   complement of `RationalMap.domain` as a `Set X`.
54	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree` (def, ~3 LOC) — predicate: every
55	   point `η : X` with `Order.coheight η = 1` lies in `f.domain`.
56	3. `Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one` (theorem, ~3 LOC) — for a smooth
57	   integral variety, the stalk at a codim-1 point is a DVR.
58	4. `Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` (theorem, ~10 LOC) —
59	   Milne 3.1 specialised to smooth source + complete target +
60	   codim-1-indeterminacy-free.
61	5. `Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`
62	   (theorem, ~8 LOC) — Milne Lemma 3.3.
63	6. `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point`
64	   (theorem, ~2 LOC) — definitional re-shuffle of `RationalMap.mem_domain`
65	   exposing a `PartialMap` representative whose domain contains `W.point`.
66	   The substantive Weil-divisor `order ≥ 0` reformulation is deferred (see
67	   header).
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	* `indeterminacyLocus f : Set X` returns the carrier of the closed
75	  complement of `f.domain` — not `∅` or `Set.univ`.
76	* `CodimOneFree f : Prop` is the genuine ∀-statement over codim-1 points,
77	  not `True`.
78	* `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one` produces a Mathlib `IsDiscreteValuationRing`
79	  instance — not a trivial proposition.
80	* `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` asserts `∃! g, g.toRationalMap = f`
81	  — the existence of a unique regular extension.
82	* `indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme` asserts a disjunction:
83	  either the locus is empty or every component has coheight `1`.
84	* `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` asserts the (truthful)
85	  iff between definedness at `W.point` and the existence of a `PartialMap`
86	  representative containing `W.point` in its domain — a definitional unfold
87	  of `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` with the
88	  conjunction swapped for downstream ergonomics.
89	
90	Unfolding any pinned declaration exposes the named substantive content
91	(the carrier set complement, the `∀ η, ...` statement, the DVR instance,
92	the unique regular extension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / empty-content
93	`proof_wanted` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` placeholders are used.
94	
95	## Variety conventions
96	
97	Following the project (cf. `Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean`,
98	`AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`), a **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** is an
99	object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:
100	
101	* `[Smooth X.hom]`, `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
102	* `[IsSeparated X.hom]`, `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
103	* `[IsIntegral X.left]`, `[IsReduced X.left]`.
104	
105	A **complete variety** adds `[IsProper Y.hom]`. A **group variety** over
106	`k̄` adds a `[GrpObj Y]` instance to the variety package.
107	
108	## References
109	
110	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_CodimOneExtension.tex` (~750 LOC,
111	6 pins). Source: Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, §I.3, Theorem 3.1 (p. 16) and
112	Lemma 3.3 (p. 17); Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II.6 pp. 130–131.
113	-/

126	
127	/-! ## §1. The indeterminacy locus of a rational map
128	
129	The indeterminacy locus `Z(f) ⊆ X` of a rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is the closed
130	complement of its domain of definition `Dom(f) := f.domain` (a Mathlib-shipped
131	open subset of `X`).
132	
133	Blueprint pin: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne §I.3 p. 16). -/
134	
135	/-- **Indeterminacy locus of a rational map.** The closed complement of the
136	domain of definition `f.domain` (an `X.Opens`, exposed by Mathlib at
137	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap`):
138	
139	`Z(f) := X ∖ Dom(f) ⊆ X`.
140	
141	`f` is everywhere defined (i.e. extends to a regular morphism) iff
142	`indeterminacyLocus f = ∅`.
143	
144	Blueprint reference: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
145	§I.3, p. 16). -/
146	def indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Set X :=
147	  (f.domain : Set X)ᶜ
148	
149	/-- The indeterminacy locus of a rational map is closed (it is the complement
150	of an open). -/
151	lemma isClosed_indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) :
152	    IsClosed (indeterminacyLocus f) := by
153	  unfold indeterminacyLocus
154	  exact (f.domain.isOpen).isClosed_compl
155	
156	/-! ## §2. Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps
157	
158	A rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is **codim-1-indeterminacy-free** if its
159	indeterminacy locus contains no prime divisor of `X`, i.e. every codim-1
160	generic point of `X` already lies in `f.domain`. Equivalently (under the
161	project's standing `Order.coheight` convention on the specialisation
162	preorder of `X.carrier`), every `η : X` with `Order.coheight η = 1` lies in
163	`f.domain`.
164	
165	Blueprint pin: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1, the
166	conclusion). -/
167	
168	/-- **Codim-1-indeterminacy-free rational map.** A rational map
169	`f : X ⇢ Y` is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if every codimension-one point
170	of `X` lies in its domain of definition. Equivalently: no prime divisor of
171	`X` (i.e. closure of a codim-1 generic point) is contained in
172	`indeterminacyLocus f`.
173	
174	The codim-1 condition is encoded via `Order.coheight η = 1` on the
175	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier`, matching the project's standing
176	convention in `Scheme.PrimeDivisor` (`RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`).
177	
178	Blueprint reference: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
179	Varieties*, Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
180	def CodimOneFree {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Prop :=
181	  ∀ (x : X), Order.coheight x = 1 → x ∈ f.domain
182	
183	end RationalMap
184	
185	/-! ## §3. Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point
186	
187	On a nonsingular variety `X` over `k̄`, the local ring at the generic point
188	of a prime divisor is a discrete valuation ring with fraction field equal to
189	the function field `K(X) = X.functionField`. This is Hartshorne II.6's
190	"regular in codimension one" property combined with the regular-local-ring-of-dim-1
191	$\Leftrightarrow$ DVR equivalence (Atiyah–Macdonald 9.2; Stacks `00PD`).
192	
193	Blueprint pin: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130). -/
194	
195	/-- **Stacks `00TT`, Jacobian-criterion direction (regularity conclusion).**
196	For a smooth integral variety `X` over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, the
197	stalk of `X` at every point is a regular local ring.
198	
199	This is the scheme-level packaging of Stacks tag `00TT` (Lemma 10.140.3
200	"Smooth algebras over fields"): given an affine chart `Spec A ⊆ X`, the
201	\(\bar k\)-algebra `A` is smooth at every prime `𝔮`, and the localisation
202	`A_𝔮 = X.presheaf.stalk z` (for `z ∈ X` lying over `𝔮`) is regular by the
203	"in this case the local ring `A_𝔮` is regular" clause of the cited lemma.
204	
205	**Mathlib status at commit `b80f227`.** The Jacobian-criterion implication
206	`Algebra.Smooth \(\bar k\) A \(\Rightarrow\) IsRegularLocalRing (Localization.AtPrime p)`
207	is not directly available, and the scheme-level packaging that promotes
208	`[Smooth X.hom]` to a stalk-wise regularity instance through the affine cover
209	+ open-immersion stalk identification is likewise absent. We capture the
210	implication as a narrow named typed `sorry` so the gap is precisely located:
211	once the algebra-side bridge and the scheme-level packaging land in Mathlib
212	(or in an upstream project file), this helper closes axiom-clean and every
213	downstream consumer (`smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`,
214	`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`) inherits the closure automatically.
215	
216	Blueprint reference: `\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}` in
217	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_CodimOneExtension.tex` (also Stacks
218	\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00TT}{tag 00TT}). -/
219	private theorem isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth
220	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
221	    (X : Over (Spec (.of kbar)))
222	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
223	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
224	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
225	    (z : X.left) :
226	    IsRegularLocalRing (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) := by
227	  -- Stacks 00TT gap: `Algebra.Smooth k̄ A → IsRegularLocalRing` at every
228	  -- prime, plus the scheme-level packaging through `presheaf.stalk` of the
229	  -- affine cover. See docstring above for the precise status.
230	  sorry
231	
232	/-- **Helper for `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`.** On a smooth integral variety
233	over an algebraically closed field, the stalk at a codimension-`1` point has a
234	*principal nonzero* maximal ideal. Encodes the substantive geometric content
235	of "regular in codimension one": smoothness ⟹ `IsRegularLocalRing` at the
236	stalk (Stacks `00PD`/`00TT`), and a Noetherian regular local ring with
237	`ringKrullDim = 1` has principal nonzero maximal ideal (Stacks `02IZ`),
238	via the cotangent-space `finrank = 1` characterisation.
239	
240	**Iter-187 Lane M↓ refactor.** The previous body bundled both Mathlib gaps —
241	smooth ⟹ regular (Stacks 00TT) and codim-1 ⟹ Krull-dim-1 — into an inline
242	`hreg_dim` conjunction whose first conjunct was a bare `sorry`. The Krull-dim
243	half has been closed axiom-clean since iter-184 via
244	`Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` (the iter-183 `CoheightBridge.lean`
245	bridge); the iter-187 refactor isolates the remaining Stacks-00TT gap as the
246	named helper `isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth` above. This helper is now
247	*axiom-clean modulo the named Stacks-00TT gap*: its body discharges every
248	conclusion from explicit Mathlib lemmas and the named regularity helper.
249	Once `isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth` closes upstream the whole chain
250	becomes axiom-clean automatically. -/
251	private theorem smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot
252	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
253	    (X : Over (Spec (.of kbar)))
254	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
255	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
256	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
257	    (z : X.left) (_hz : Order.coheight z = 1) :
258	    Submodule.IsPrincipal (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (X.left.presheaf.stalk z)) ∧
259	      IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) ≠ ⊥ := by
260	  -- Set up Noetherian structure on the stalk (needed for the cotangent-space API):
261	  -- `LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom` lifts the automatic `IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k̄)`
262	  -- through to `IsLocallyNoetherian X.left`, from which the stalk picks up
263	  -- `IsNoetherianRing` (Mathlib instance).
264	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian X.left :=
265	    LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian X.hom
266	  -- Regularity half: extracted to the named helper above
267	  -- (`isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth`), which captures the Stacks-00TT
268	  -- Jacobian-criterion gap as a narrow named typed sorry. No further
269	  -- inline `sorry` is required at this step.
270	  have hreg : IsRegularLocalRing (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) :=
271	    isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth X z
272	  -- Krull-dim half: closes via the iter-183 axiom-clean CoheightBridge
273	  -- equality + the codim-1 witness `_hz`.
274	  have hdim : ringKrullDim (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) = 1 := by
275	    rw [Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight]
276	    exact_mod_cast _hz
277	  -- From regularity + dim = 1, the cotangent space has `finrank = 1`
278	  -- (Mathlib `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace`).
279	  have hfin :
280	      Module.finrank
281	        (IsLocalRing.ResidueField (X.left.presheaf.stalk z))
282	        (IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace (X.left.presheaf.stalk z)) = 1 := by
283	    have h := (IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace _).mp hreg
284	    rw [hdim] at h
285	    exact_mod_cast h
286	  -- Principal maximal ideal: `finrank ≤ 1 ↔ IsPrincipal maximalIdeal`
287	  -- (Mathlib `IsLocalRing.finrank_cotangentSpace_le_one_iff`).
288	  have hprin : Submodule.IsPrincipal
289	      (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (X.left.presheaf.stalk z)) :=
290	    IsLocalRing.finrank_cotangentSpace_le_one_iff.mp hfin.le
291	  -- `maximalIdeal ≠ ⊥`: equivalent to "not a field" by Mathlib
292	  -- `IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq`, and a field has Krull dim 0,
293	  -- contradicting `hdim : ringKrullDim = 1` via `ringKrullDim_eq_zero_of_isField`.
294	  have hne : IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) ≠ ⊥ := by
295	    intro hbot
296	    have hF : IsField (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) :=
297	      IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq.mpr hbot
298	    have h0 : ringKrullDim (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) = 0 :=
299	      ringKrullDim_eq_zero_of_isField hF
300	    -- Contradiction: `1 = 0` in `WithBot ℕ∞`.
301	    rw [hdim] at h0
302	    exact_mod_cast h0
303	  exact ⟨hprin, hne⟩
304	
305	/-- **Smooth + codim-1 ⇒ DVR.** For a nonsingular integral variety `X` over
306	an algebraically closed field `k̄` and a point `η : X.left` with
307	`Order.coheight η = 1`, the stalk `X.left.presheaf.stalk η` is a discrete
308	valuation ring (with fraction field the function field `K(X)`).
309	
310	The proof reduces to "regular local ring of Krull dimension `1` is a DVR"
311	(Mathlib `IsDiscreteValuationRing.isDiscreteValuationRing_iff` or analogous)
312	plus the smooth-variety fact that every local ring at a codim-1 point is
313	regular of dimension `1`. The geometric step is packaged in the private helper
314	`smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`; the rest assembles the
315	DVR instance via `IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE`.
316	
317	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130;
318	Stacks `00PD` for the regular-of-dim-1 ⇔ DVR equivalence). -/
319	theorem localRing_dvr_of_codim_one
320	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
321	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
322	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
323	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
324	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
325	    (z : X.left) (_hz : Order.coheight z = 1) :
326	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) := by
327	  -- Set up Noetherian structure on the stalk:
328	  -- `LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom` lifts the (automatic) `IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k̄)`
329	  -- to `IsLocallyNoetherian X.left`, from which the stalk inherits
330	  -- `IsNoetherianRing` (Mathlib instance). The stalk of an integral scheme is
331	  -- a domain (Mathlib instance), and stalks of schemes are always local (Mathlib
332	  -- instance); these are picked up automatically by typeclass search.
333	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian X.left :=
334	    LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian X.hom
335	  -- Extract the principal+nonzero-maximal-ideal helper (the Mathlib-gap content).
336	  obtain ⟨hprin, hne⟩ :=
337	    smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot X z _hz
338	  -- The stalk is a local Noetherian domain with principal maximal ideal:
339	  -- promote `IsPrincipal (maximalIdeal)` to a `IsPrincipalIdealRing` instance
340	  -- and conclude DVR via `IsDiscreteValuationRing.mk`.
341	  -- The TFAE entry "principal maximal ideal ⇔ DVR" handles the conversion
342	  -- once we know `¬ IsField` (which follows from `maximalIdeal ≠ ⊥`).
343	  have hfield : ¬ IsField (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) := by
344	    intro hF
345	    exact hne ((IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq).mp hF)
346	  have tfae := IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) hfield
347	  -- TFAE entry index 4 is `Submodule.IsPrincipal (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)`.
348	  exact ((tfae.out 0 4).mpr hprin)

352	/-! ## §4. Milne Theorem 3.1 — codim-≥ 2 extension to a complete target
353	
354	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a complete variety has
355	indeterminacy locus of codimension `≥ 2`. Specialising further: if `f` is
356	already codim-1-indeterminacy-free (by hypothesis), then `Dom(f) = X` and
357	`f` extends to a unique regular morphism `X ⟶ Y`.
358	
359	Blueprint pin: `thm:codim_one_extension` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1 p. 16). -/
360	
361	/-- **Milne Theorem 3.1 (specialised) — codim-1-indeterminacy-free + smooth
362	source + complete target ⇒ extension.**
363	
364	For `X` a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
365	`Y` a complete variety over `k̄`, and `f : X ⇢ Y` a codim-1-indeterminacy-free
366	rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism `g : X ⟶ Y`
367	whose induced rational map equals `f`.
368	
369	The proof combines two steps:
370	* **Step 1** (codim-1 ruling-out): the indeterminacy locus has no codim-1
371	  components, because at the generic point of any candidate prime divisor
372	  `W ⊆ Z(f)` the DVR property of the stalk (`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`)
373	  feeds the valuative criterion of properness on the complete target `Y`.
374	* **Step 2** (codim-≥2 extension): at a remaining codim-≥2 point `x`, the
375	  depth-≥2 property of the regular local ring `O_{X,x}` (Cohen–Macaulay
376	  from `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` of `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean`)
377	  forces `H¹_{x}(V, O_X) = 0` and the pulled-back coordinates extend
378	  uniquely. By the `CodimOneFree` hypothesis, Step 1 alone is already
379	  enough on the dim-≤2 cases the project consumes; Step 2 is what extends
380	  through the remaining higher-codim points to obtain `Dom(f) = X`.
381	
382	Uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
383	(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`): two morphisms `X ⟶ Y` that agree
384	on a dense open of the reduced scheme `X` agree everywhere.
385	
386	Blueprint reference: `thm:codim_one_extension` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
387	Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
388	theorem extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth
389	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
390	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
391	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
392	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
393	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
394	    {Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
395	    [IsProper Y.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]
396	    [IsSeparated Y.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType Y.hom]
397	    [IsIntegral Y.left] [IsReduced Y.left]
398	    (f : X.left.RationalMap Y.left) (_hf : CodimOneFree f) :
399	    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ Y.left), g.toRationalMap = f :=
400	  sorry
401	
402	/-! ## §5. Milne Lemma 3.3 — pure-codim-1 indeterminacy into a group variety
403	
404	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety has its
405	indeterminacy locus either empty or of pure codimension `1`. Combined with
406	`thm:codim_one_extension`'s codim-≥2 conclusion, this forces the locus to
407	be empty when the target is an abelian variety.
408	
409	Blueprint pin: `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Lemma 3.3 p. 17). -/
410	
411	/-- **Milne Lemma 3.3 — indeterminacy into a group variety is empty or pure
412	codim 1.**
413	
414	For `X` a nonsingular variety over `k̄`, `G` a group variety over `k̄`
415	(a smooth group scheme of finite type, separated), and `f : X ⇢ G` a
416	rational map, the indeterminacy locus `Z(f) := indeterminacyLocus f` is
417	either empty (`f` is defined on all of `X`) or every point of `Z(f)` lies in
418	the closure of a codim-1 generic point.
419	
420	The proof (Milne's): consider the difference map
421	`Φ : X × X ⇢ G`, `(x, y) ↦ f(x) · f(y)⁻¹`. Then `Φ` is defined at `(x, x)`
422	iff `f` is defined at `x`. Pulling back the maximal ideal of `O_{G,e}` gives
423	a finite set of rational functions on `X × X` whose pole divisors are pure
424	codim-1; their intersection with the diagonal exhibits the indeterminacy
425	locus of `f` as pure codim-1.
426	
427	Blueprint reference: `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
428	Varieties*, Lemma 3.3, §I.3, p. 17). -/
429	theorem indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme
430	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
431	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
432	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
433	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
434	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
435	    {G : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
436	    [GrpObj G] [Smooth G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom]
437	    [IsSeparated G.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]
438	    [IsIntegral G.left] [IsReduced G.left]
439	    (f : X.left.RationalMap G.left) :
440	    indeterminacyLocus f = ∅ ∨
441	      ∀ x ∈ indeterminacyLocus f,
442	        ∃ (z : X.left), Order.coheight z = 1 ∧ x ∈ closure ({z} : Set X.left) :=
443	  sorry
