content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	[Flasque sheaf]
74	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
75	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
76	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
77	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
78	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
79	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
80	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
82	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
83	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
84	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
85	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
86	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
87	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
88	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
89	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
90	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
91	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
92	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
93	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
94	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
95	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
96	
97	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
98	  applied to a sheaf
99	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
100	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
101	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
102	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
103	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
104	
105	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
106	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
107	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
108	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
109	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
110	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
111	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
112	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
113	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
114	\end{definition}
115	
116	\begin{lemma}
117	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
118	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
120	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
121	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
122	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
123	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
124	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
125	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
126	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
127	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
128	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
129	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
130	\end{lemma}
131	
132	\begin{proof}
133	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
134	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
135	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
136	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
137	  \(X\):
138	  \[
139	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
140	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
141	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
142	  \]
143	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
144	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
145	  is flasque on \(Y\).
146	\end{proof}
147	
148	\begin{lemma}
149	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
150	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
152	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
153	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
154	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
155	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
156	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
157	  % topological spaces."
158	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
159	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
160	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
161	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
162	\end{lemma}
163	
164	\begin{proof}
165	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
166	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
167	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
168	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
169	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
170	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
171	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
172	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
173	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
174	  flasque.
175	\end{proof}
176	
177	\begin{theorem}
178	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
179	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
181	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
182	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
183	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
184	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
185	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
186	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
187	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
188	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
189	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
190	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
191	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
192	  \[
193	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
194	  \]
195	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
196	\end{theorem}
197	
198	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
199	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
200	% be the quotient:
201	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
202	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
203	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
204	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
205	% Ex. 1.16b)
206	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
207	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
208	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
209	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
210	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
211	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
212	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
213	% \(i\) we get the result."
214	\begin{proof}
215	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
216	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
217	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
218	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
219	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
220	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
221	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
222	  short exact sequence
223	  \[
224	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
225	  \]
226	  in the sheaf category.
227	
228	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
229	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
230	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
231	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
232	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
233	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
234	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
235	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
236	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
237	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
238	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
239	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
240	
241	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
242	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
243	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
244	  exact sequence on global sections
245	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
246	  \[
247	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
248	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
249	  \]
250	
251	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
252	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
253	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
254	  positive degree:
255	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
256	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
257	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
258	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
259	  \[
260	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
261	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
262	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
263	  \]
264	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
265	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
266	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
267	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
268	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
269	
270	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
271	  exact sequence yields
272	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
273	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
274	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
275	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
276	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
277	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
278	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
279	
280	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
281	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
282	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
283	  % already provided by chapter
284	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
285	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
286	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
287	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
288	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
289	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
290	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
291	  % LOC apiece.
292	\end{proof}

294	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
295	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
296	
297	\begin{lemma}
298	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
299	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
300	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
301	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
302	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
303	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
304	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
305	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
306	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
307	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
308	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
309	  % X\) is the inclusion."
310	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
311	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
312	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
313	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
314	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
315	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
316	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
317	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
318	\end{lemma}
319	
320	\begin{proof}
321	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
322	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
323	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
324	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
325	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
326	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
327	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
328	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
329	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
330	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
331	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
332	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
333	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
334	\end{proof}
335	
336	\begin{lemma}
337	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
338	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
340	
341	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
342	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
343	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
344	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
345	
346	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
347	  no external source pinned).
348	\end{lemma}
349	
350	\begin{proof}
351	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
352	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
353	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
354	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
355	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
356	\end{proof}
357	
358	\begin{lemma}
359	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
360	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
362	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
363	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
364	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
365	
366	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
367	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
368	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
369	  \[
370	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
371	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
372	  \]
373	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
374	
375	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
376	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
377	  above).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
382	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
383	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
384	  By
385	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
386	  isomorphic to the pushforward
387	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
388	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
389	  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
390	  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
391	  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
392	  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
393	  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
394	  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the
395	  isomorphism of \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, so
396	  \(k(P)\) is flasque.
397	\end{proof}
398	
399	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
400	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
401	
402	\begin{theorem}
403	
404	\leanok
405	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
406	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
408	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
409	
410	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
411	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
412	  \[
413	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
414	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
415	  \]
416	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
417	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
418	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
419	
420	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
421	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
422	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
423	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
424	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
425	  above.
426	\end{theorem}
427	
428	\begin{proof}
429	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
430	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
431	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
432	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
433	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
434	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
435	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
436	  \[
437	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
438	  \]
439	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
440	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
441	\end{proof}

526	\section{Out of scope}
527	
528	The following items lie outside this chapter and are scheduled into
529	sibling sub-builds:
530	\begin{itemize}
531	  \item \emph{General coherent-sheaf cohomology vanishing.} Higher
532	    cohomology vanishing for general coherent sheaves on a curve
533	    requires Grothendieck vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme
534	    (Hartshorne~III.2.7), which the chapter does \emph{not} use; the
535	    chapter is narrowly tailored to the flasque/skyscraper case
536	    \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) that the genus-\(0\) critical path needs.
537	  \item \emph{Serre duality for curves.} The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch
538	    statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) routes through
539	    Serre duality \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
540	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*\); Mathlib has neither the dualizing sheaf
541	    \(\omega_C\) on a scheme nor the duality theorem. The chapter
542	    sidesteps duality entirely by using flasqueness directly (the
543	    Hartshorne~III~\S2 order of presentation, not the
544	    Hartshorne~III~\S7 one).
545	  \item \emph{The \(H^0\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).} The
546	    one-dimensionality identification \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) is the
547	    sibling substrate lemma
548	    REF of
549	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, closed axiom-clean iter-188
550	    against the project's four-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain.
551	    The present chapter is the complementary H\(^1\) half.
552	  \item \emph{The Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b),(c) inputs.} The proof
553	    of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} consumes two named Hartshorne
554	    inputs as black-box facts (1.16(b): the global-sections functor is
555	    exact on a short exact sequence whose leftmost term is flasque;
556	    1.16(c): the quotient of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence
557	    is flasque). These are project-side ancillary lemmas; they are
558	    sketched inside the proof of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} but
559	    not given their own \texttt{\textbackslash{}lemma} block here. If the
560	    iter-191+ prover finds a substantive reason to factor either out, a
561	    follow-up blueprint round will promote it to its own pin.
562	\end{itemize}

9	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.OcOfD
10	
11	/-!
12	# The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)
13	
14	This file is the **RR.2** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
15	headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically
16	irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
17	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
18	
19	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
20	through:
21	
22	- `RR.1` (`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` / `WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil
23	  divisor group `Div(C)` and the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
24	- **`RR.2` (this file, `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`)**: the Riemann–Roch
25	  dimension formula `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0` (with `deg D ≥ 0`),
26	  via the auxiliary Euler-characteristic identity
27	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`.
28	- `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, future): the invertible sheaf
29	  `𝒪_C(D)`, the linear-equivalence isomorphism `𝒪_C(D) ≅ 𝒪_C(D')` for
30	  `D ∼ D'`, and the `H¹`-vanishing input
31	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0` for `deg D ≥ 0` on a genus-`0` curve.
32	- `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-174 Lane F file-skeleton)
36	
37	This file is the **iter-174 Lane F** file-skeleton: each of the four pinned
38	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
39	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). The
40	Euler-characteristic carrier definition is concrete (a one-line subtraction
41	of `H⁰` and `H¹` `Module.finrank`s, mirroring the `genus` definition of
42	`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`); the remaining pins carry `sorry` bodies whose
43	closure is iter-175+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.3`
44	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`) and `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
45	
46	The 4 pinned declarations are:
47	
48	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic` — Euler characteristic
49	   `χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)` of a `ModuleCat k̄`-valued
50	   sheaf on `C` (the curve specialisation of the alternating sum, since
51	   `H^i = 0` for `i ≥ 2` on a one-dimensional scheme by Grothendieck
52	   vanishing).
53	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l` — the `ℓ`-invariant
54	   `ℓ(D) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D))` of a Weil divisor `D`.
55	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
56	   — the Euler-characteristic identity `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` for
57	   every `D ∈ Div(C)` on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
58	   `C / k̄` of genus `g = g(C)`.
59	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero`
60	   — the Riemann–Roch formula in genus `0`: `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` for any
61	   Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)` with `deg D ≥ 0` on a smooth proper
62	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0` (threading
63	   the `H¹`-vanishing of `𝒪_C(D)` explicitly as a named premise until
64	   `RR.3` lands).
65	
66	## Note on `𝒪_C(D)` (the invertible sheaf of a divisor)
67	
68	The chapter's proof of `eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
69	and the statement of `l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero` both reference
70	the line bundle `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D`. Mathlib `b80f227` ships no
71	`Scheme.lineBundleOfDivisor` (the closest is `WeierstrassCurve.lineBundle`
72	in the elliptic-curve formalisation), and the project-side construction of
73	`𝒪_C(D)` is queued for `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). To keep the type
74	signatures of pins 2–4 substantive in the iter-174 skeleton, we expose a
75	**typed-`sorry` placeholder**
76	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` that pairs each divisor with
77	the `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf carrier `𝒪_C(D)` is intended to occupy. The
78	iter-175+ closure of `RR.3` replaces this placeholder's body with the
79	honest invertible-sheaf construction; the present pins consume it only
80	through its `H⁰` and `H¹` cohomology, so the consumer signatures are
81	substantive in the type sense (each asserts an arithmetic identity on the
82	finiteness-of-`H^*` outputs).
83	
84	## References
85	
86	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex` (Hartshorne
87	IV.1 verbatim quotes; 4 pins).
88	Source: Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, IV §1 (pp. 294–297), Theorem 1.3
89	(Riemann–Roch) and Example 1.3.5 (genus-`0` specialisation). Stacks Project
90	tags 0BSC (Euler characteristic on a curve), 0AYO (Riemann–Roch).
91	-/
92	
93	set_option autoImplicit false
94	
95	universe u
96	
97	open CategoryTheory Limits
98	
99	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
100

440	  -- the dite branch picks the value `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar`.
441	  change Module.finrank kbar
442	    ((skyscraperPresheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
443	      (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
444	      (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) = 1
445	  rw [skyscraperPresheaf_obj]
446	  simp only [Opposite.unop_op]
447	  exact (if_pos trivial).symm ▸ Module.finrank_self kbar
448	
449	/-- **H¹ vanishing for a closed-point skyscraper** (Lane H sub-helper —
450	iter-188).
451	
452	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar`, the first
453	cohomology of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes:
454	`dim_{kbar} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`.
455	
456	The mathematical content: the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is the
457	pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space (Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)),
458	hence flasque, and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology on
459	any topological space (Hartshorne~III.2.5).
460	
461	**iter-188 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The body waits
462	on the project-side `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured flasque-cohomology bridge
463	(or upstream Mathlib `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` for
464	`Abelian.Ext`-cohomology sheaves with values in an abelian category with
465	enough injectives). The committed `RR.2.H¹` sub-phase of STRATEGY.md
466	schedules this body (~150-300 LOC across ~8-12 iters; off the iter-188
467	H⁰ critical-path target). -/
468	private theorem Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero
469	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
470	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
471	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
472	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
473	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
474	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
475	    Module.finrank kbar
476	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
477	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
478	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 1) = 0 := by
479	  sorry
480	
481	/-- **χ of the closed-point skyscraper is 1** (Lane H sub-helper —
482	iter-186).
483	
484	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar`, the
485	Euler characteristic of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
486	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` is `1`:
487	`χ(k(P)) = dim H⁰(C, k(P)) − dim H¹(C, k(P)) = 1 − 0 = 1`.
488	
489	**iter-188 Lane H status** — assembled axiom-clean modulo the H¹ named
490	typed-sorry helper `H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` (gated on the
491	project-side flasque-cohomology bridge — see that helper's docstring).
492	The H⁰ half is closed axiom-clean by
493	`H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one`. -/
494	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf
495	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
496	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
497	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
498	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
499	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
500	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
501	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
502	        (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
503	          (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) = 1 := by
504	  -- Unfold χ = dim H⁰ − dim H¹ and substitute the two halves.
505	  unfold Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
506	  -- Renormalise the goal with explicit `(C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)` so the
507	  -- helper-conclusion `Module.finrank` matches under the `↑(·)` casts.
508	  change ((Module.finrank kbar
509	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
510	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
511	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 0) : ℕ) : ℤ)
512	      - ((Module.finrank kbar
513	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
514	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
515	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 1) : ℕ) : ℤ) = 1
516	  rw [Scheme.H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one C P,
517	    Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero C P]
518	  -- Arithmetic: (1 : ℤ) − (0 : ℤ) = 1.
519	  simp
520	
521	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_of_shortExact_skyscraper
522	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
523	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
524	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
525	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
526	    (S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
527	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
528	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)))
529	    (_hSE : S.ShortExact)
530	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
531	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)]
532	    (_h13 : Nonempty (S.X₃ ≅ skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
533	      P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))) :
534	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C S.X₂
535	      = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C S.X₁ + 1 := by
536	  -- Assembly from the 3 substantive helpers:
537	  --   χ(X₂) = χ(X₁) + χ(X₃)               (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add)
538	  --        = χ(X₁) + χ(skyscraperSheaf …)  (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso via _h13)
539	  --        = χ(X₁) + 1                     (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf)

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2026 Christian Merten. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Christian Merten
5	-/
6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK.SheafProperty
7	
8	/-!
9	# Sheaves of `k`-modules: finite-length carriers and producer instances
10	
11	Sub-file (3/3) of the `StructureSheafModuleK` split (refactor iter-174). This file
12	hosts the downstream finite-length carriers (`IsAffineHModuleVanishing`,
13	`IsHModuleHomFinite`), their algebraic bridges (the `HModule` / `HModule'`
14	abbrevs, the H⁰ `LinearEquiv` transport companions, the constant-sheaf-Γ adjunction
15	upgrade), and the curve-side producer instances. Upstream sub-files
16	`Presheaf.lean` and `SheafProperty.lean` provide the structure-sheaf object the
17	carriers depend on.
18	
19	Downstream finite-length carriers in this file:
20	
21	* H>0 affine cohomology vanishing is carried by iter-040's
22	  `IsAffineHModuleVanishing` (the producer instance for the curve case
23	  lives at iter-051).
24	* H⁰ Hom-finiteness is carried by iter-043's **wholespace**
25	  `IsHModuleHomFinite`; the producer instance on a proper geometrically
26	  integral $k$-curve is supplied at iter-046 from Stein finiteness
27	  (`module_finite_globalSections_of_isProper`).
28	* The iter-041 per-affine-open variant `IsAffineHModuleHomFinite` was an
29	  abandoned attempt (dead scaffolding — $\Gamma(U, O_C)$ is not finite
30	  over $k$ on a proper affine open of a non-trivial curve) and has been
31	  removed from this file.
32	
33	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`.
34	-/
35	
36	set_option autoImplicit false
37	
38	universe u v
39	
40	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry
41	
42	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
43	
44	/-- Phase A step 5/6 bridge (iter-009 scaffold): the parallel `Sheaf.H` for
45	`ModuleCat k`-valued sheaves. Mathlib's `CategoryTheory.Sheaf.H` is
46	parameterised over `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat` only, so closing `genus` honestly
47	requires this `ModuleCat k`-flavoured version. The result carries `Module k`
48	automatically via `CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.instModule`, and
49	`Module.finrank k` is therefore well-defined on it. The declaration is a
50	`noncomputable abbrev` (rather than `def`) so that instance synthesis sees
51	through the wrapper to find `Module k` and `AddCommGroup` instances. -/
52	noncomputable abbrev HModule
53	    (k : Type u) [Field k]
54	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
55	    [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)] [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
56	    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (n : ℕ) : Type (u+1) :=
57	  Abelian.Ext ((constantSheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)).obj (ModuleCat.of k k)) F n
58	
59	/-- Phase A step 6 algebraic bridge (iter-010 scaffold): the $k$-linear
60	identification of `HModule k F 0` with the Hom group from the constant
61	sheaf at `ModuleCat.of k k`. Mathlib provides
62	`CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀ : Ext X Y 0 ≃ₗ[R] (X ⟶ Y)` in any
63	`Linear R`-enriched abelian category; specialised to the `Linear k`
64	enrichment of `Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)` (auto-inferable from
65	`HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)`), this collapses `HModule k F 0` to a
66	`k`-linear Hom group. The closure body is `Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀`;
67	probe-confirmed one-liner (iter-010 plan-agent). Used downstream to
68	identify `H⁰(C, toModuleKSheaf C)` with `Γ(C, O_C)` viewed as a
69	`k`-module on a connected proper `k`-curve. -/
70	noncomputable def HModule_zero_linearEquiv
71	    (k : Type u) [Field k]
72	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
73	    [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)] [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
74	    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) :
75	    HModule k F 0 ≃ₗ[k]
76	      ((constantSheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)).obj (ModuleCat.of k k) ⟶ F) :=
77	  Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀
78	
79	/-- Phase A step 6 *Path 2* (iter-013 scaffold): the `ModuleCat k`-flavored
80	cohomology of an open `X : C` with values in a sheaf `F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)`.
81	Mirrors Mathlib's `Sheaf.H' F n X = (F.cohomologyPresheaf n).obj (op X)`
82	(`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/Basic.lean` L105) for
83	`AddCommGrpCat`-valued sheaves, with `AddCommGrpCat.free → ModuleCat.free k`.
84	
85	The codomain is `Type u` (not `ModuleCat.{u} k`): `Abelian.Ext` returns a bare
86	`Type u` carrying `Module k` via `Abelian.Ext.instModule` through the `Linear k`
87	enrichment. The `noncomputable abbrev` form (rather than `def`) is required so
88	instance synthesis sees through the wrapper to find `Module k (HModule' k F n X)`
89	and `AddCommGroup (HModule' k F n X)` — under `def`, `Module.finrank` would fail
90	to typecheck (cf. iter-009 design rationale on `HModule`).
91	
92	This is the prerequisite for the iter-014+ `ModuleCat k`-flavored Mayer-Vietoris
93	LES (mirror of `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/MayerVietoris.lean`).
94	The iter-014+ work will state and prove the LES on a `MayerVietorisSquare`,
95	specialising to a 2-affine cover of a proper `k`-curve in iter-015+. The
96	comparison theorem `cechCohomology_OC C 𝒰 n ≅ HModule k (toModuleKSheaf C) n`
97	for an acyclic cover (the technical heart of Path-2) is also queued for
98	iter-015+. -/
99	noncomputable abbrev HModule' (k : Type u) [Field k]
100	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
101	    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
102	    [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]

80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor

119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122

125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised

388	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar`, the global

390	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` form a one-dimensional

395	chain — `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` (Ext₀ ≃ Hom-from-constant-sheaf) +
396	`constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv` (constant-sheaf/Γ adjunction) +

401	skyscraper-presheaf evaluates at `⊤` to its value `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar`,
402	whose `kbar`-finrank is `1` by `Module.finrank_self`. -/
403	private theorem Scheme.H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one
404	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
405	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
406	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]

409	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :

413	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 0) = 1 := by
414	  -- The four-step `kbar`-linear-equivalence chain identifying H⁰ with the
415	  -- presheaf evaluation at the top open (mirrors the structure-sheaf chain
416	  -- of `finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one`).
417	  let LE1 :=
418	    Scheme.HModule_zero_linearEquiv kbar
419	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
420	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))
421	  let LE2 :=
422	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv kbar
423	      (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
424	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
425	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))
426	  let LE3 :=
427	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.homFromOne_linearEquiv kbar
428	      ((Sheaf.Γ (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
429	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj
430	        (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
431	          (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)))
432	  let LE4 :=
433	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top kbar
434	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
435	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))
436	  let LE := ((LE1.trans LE2).trans LE3).trans LE4
437	  rw [LE.finrank_eq]
438	  -- Goal: Module.finrank kbar ((skyscraperSheaf ...).obj.obj (op ⊤)) = 1.
439	  -- Reduce the skyscraper-presheaf evaluation at ⊤: since P.point ∈ ⊤,
440	  -- the dite branch picks the value `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar`.

442	    ((skyscraperPresheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
443	      (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
444	      (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) = 1
445	  rw [skyscraperPresheaf_obj]

449	/-- **H¹ vanishing for a closed-point skyscraper** (Lane H sub-helper —
450	iter-188).
451	
452	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar`, the first
453	cohomology of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes:
454	`dim_{kbar} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`.
455	
456	The mathematical content: the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is the
457	pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space (Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)),
458	hence flasque, and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology on
459	any topological space (Hartshorne~III.2.5).
460	
461	**iter-188 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The body waits
462	on the project-side `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured flasque-cohomology bridge
463	(or upstream Mathlib `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` for
464	`Abelian.Ext`-cohomology sheaves with values in an abelian category with
465	enough injectives). The committed `RR.2.H¹` sub-phase of STRATEGY.md
466	schedules this body (~150-300 LOC across ~8-12 iters; off the iter-188
467	H⁰ critical-path target). -/
468	private theorem Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero

101	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
102	
103	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
104	curve `C / k̄`.**
105	
106	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
107	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
108	alternating sum
109	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
110	expression
111	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
112	
113	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
114	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
115	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
116	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
117	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
118	
119	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
120	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
121	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
122	
123	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
124	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
125	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
126	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
127	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
128	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
129	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
130	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
131	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
132	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
133	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
134	
135	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (sibling chapter)
136	
137	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
138	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean`),
139	where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of closed points are glued
140	into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. **iter-183 Lane K** opened that file
141	with the typed-`sorry` pin `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` plus the three
142	immediate corollaries `sheafOf_zero`, `sheafOf_singlePoint`, and
143	`sheafOf_ses_single_add`. **iter-183 Lane H** (this file) retires the
144	former local `sheafOf` typed-`sorry` placeholder (previously at L168 in
145	the iter-174 skeleton) by importing `OcOfD.lean`; downstream pins (the
146	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) now
147	reference the chapter's canonical `sheafOf` directly. -/
148	
149	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
150	
151	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
152	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
153	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
154	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
155	
156	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
157	
158	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
159	`C / k̄`.**
160	
161	By definition,
162	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
163	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
164	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
165	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
166	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
167	of `genus C`.
168	
169	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
170	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
171	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
172	
173	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
174	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
175	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
176	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
177	
178	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
179	
180	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
181

184	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
185	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
186	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of

188	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style

190	body.
191	
192	**Iter-181 Lane H factoring.** Both Hartshorne IV.1.3 inputs (the base

12	/-!
13	# Skyscraper (pre)sheaves
14	
15	A skyscraper (pre)sheaf `𝓕 : (Pre)Sheaf C X` is the (pre)sheaf with value `A` at point `p₀` that is
16	supported only at open sets contain `p₀`, i.e. `𝓕(U) = A` if `p₀ ∈ U` and `𝓕(U) = *` if `p₀ ∉ U`
17	where `*` is a terminal object of `C`. In terms of stalks, `𝓕` is supported at all specializations
18	of `p₀`, i.e. if `p₀ ⤳ x` then `𝓕ₓ ≅ A` and if `¬ p₀ ⤳ x` then `𝓕ₓ ≅ *`.
19	
20	## Main definitions
21	
22	* `skyscraperPresheaf`: `skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A` is the skyscraper presheaf at point `p₀` with
23	  value `A`.
24	* `skyscraperSheaf`: the skyscraper presheaf satisfies the sheaf condition.
25	
26	## Main statements
27	
28	* `skyscraperPresheafStalkOfSpecializes`: if `y ∈ closure {p₀}` then the stalk of
29	  `skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A` at `y` is `A`.
30	* `skyscraperPresheafStalkOfNotSpecializes`: if `y ∉ closure {p₀}` then the stalk of
31	  `skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A` at `y` is `*` the terminal object.
32	
33	TODO: generalize universe level when calculating stalks, after generalizing universe level of stalk.
34	TODO(@joelriou): refactor the definitions in this file so as to make them
35	particular cases of general constructions for points of sites from
36	`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/Skyscraper.lean`.
37	
38	-/
39

41	
42	noncomputable section
43	
44	open TopologicalSpace TopCat CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Opposite
45	open scoped AlgebraicGeometry
46	
47	universe u v w
48	
49	variable {X : TopCat.{u}} (p₀ : X) [∀ U : Opens X, Decidable (p₀ ∈ U)]
50	
51	section

53	variable {C : Type v} [Category.{w} C] [HasTerminal C] (A : C)
54	
55	/-- A skyscraper presheaf is a presheaf supported at a single point: if `p₀ ∈ X` is a specified
56	point, then the skyscraper presheaf `𝓕` with value `A` is defined by `U ↦ A` if `p₀ ∈ U` and
57	`U ↦ *` if `p₀ ∉ A` where `*` is some terminal object.
58	-/
59	@[simps]
60	def skyscraperPresheaf : Presheaf C X where
61	  obj U := if p₀ ∈ unop U then A else terminal C
62	  map {U V} i :=
63	    if h : p₀ ∈ unop V then eqToHom <| by rw [if_pos h, if_pos (by simpa using i.unop.le h)]
64	    else ((if_neg h).symm.ndrec terminalIsTerminal).from _
65	  map_id U :=
66	    (em (p₀ ∈ U.unop)).elim (fun h => dif_pos h) fun h =>
67	      ((if_neg h).symm.ndrec terminalIsTerminal).hom_ext _ _
68	  map_comp {U V W} iVU iWV := by
69	    by_cases hW : p₀ ∈ unop W
70	    · have hV : p₀ ∈ unop V := leOfHom iWV.unop hW
71	      simp only [dif_pos hW, dif_pos hV, eqToHom_trans]
72	    · dsimp; rw [dif_neg hW]; apply ((if_neg hW).symm.ndrec terminalIsTerminal).hom_ext
73	
74	theorem skyscraperPresheaf_eq_pushforward
75	    [hd : ∀ U : Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}), Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)] :
76	    skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A =
77	      (ofHom (ContinuousMap.const (TopCat.of PUnit) p₀)) _*
78	        skyscraperPresheaf (X := TopCat.of PUnit) PUnit.unit A := by
79	  convert_to @skyscraperPresheaf X p₀ (fun U => hd <| (Opens.map <| ofHom <|
80	      ContinuousMap.const _ p₀).obj U)

221	/-- If `y ∉ closure {p₀}`, then the stalk of `skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A` at `y` is a terminal object
222	-/
223	def skyscraperPresheafStalkOfNotSpecializesIsTerminal [HasColimits C] {y : X} (h : ¬p₀ ⤳ y) :
224	    IsTerminal ((skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A).stalk y) :=
225	  IsTerminal.ofIso terminalIsTerminal <| (skyscraperPresheafStalkOfNotSpecializes _ _ h).symm
226	
227	theorem skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf : (skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A).IsSheaf := by
228	  classical exact
229	    (Presheaf.isSheaf_iso_iff (eqToIso <| skyscraperPresheaf_eq_pushforward p₀ A)).mpr <|
230	      (Sheaf.pushforward_sheaf_of_sheaf _
231	        (Presheaf.isSheaf_on_punit_of_isTerminal _ (by
232	          dsimp [skyscraperPresheaf]
233	          rw [if_neg]
234	          · exact terminalIsTerminal
235	          · #adaptation_note /-- 2024-03-24
236	            Previously the universe annotation was not needed here. -/
237	            exact Set.notMem_empty PUnit.unit.{u + 1})))
238	
239	/--
240	The skyscraper presheaf supported at `p₀` with value `A` is the sheaf that assigns `A` to all opens
241	`U` that contain `p₀` and assigns `*` otherwise.
242	-/
243	def skyscraperSheaf : Sheaf C X :=
244	  ⟨skyscraperPresheaf p₀ A, skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf _ _⟩
245	
246	/-- Taking skyscraper sheaf at a point is functorial: `c ↦ skyscraper p₀ c` defines a functor by
247	sending every `f : a ⟶ b` to the natural transformation `α` defined as: `α(U) = f : a ⟶ b` if
248	`p₀ ∈ U` and the unique morphism to a terminal object in `C` if `p₀ ∉ U`.
249	-/
250	def skyscraperSheafFunctor : C ⥤ Sheaf C X where
251	  obj c := skyscraperSheaf p₀ c
252	  map f := ObjectProperty.homMk <| (skyscraperPresheafFunctor p₀).map f
253	  map_id _ := Sheaf.hom_ext <| (skyscraperPresheafFunctor p₀).map_id _
254	  map_comp _ _ := Sheaf.hom_ext <| (skyscraperPresheafFunctor p₀).map_comp _ _

6	module
